segments.s229-g9: بخواهم is a first‑person volitional/subjunctive form meaning “I wish / I want” and appears first by poetic inversion. کنون is an adverb “now.” کین is a noun “vengeance / hatred” serving as the object of بخواهم. کاموس خوار is a compound epithet placed after کین; its exact lexical identity is archaic/poetic (see senses).
segments.s229-g10: اگر باشدم is a protasis using an older subjunctive/optative form (باشـ- + ـدم) meaning “if I should be / if I am to be.” زین is the noun “saddle” (here adverbial: ‘in/on the saddle’), سپس is an adverb “afterward / thereafter,” and کارزار is “battle / fight”; together this clause expresses the condition under which the speaker intends the vengeance.
segments.s229-g11: چو is a temporal conjunction “when.” گفتار ساوه is a genitive phrase “the speech/words of Saveh.” برستم here is a proper name (Rostam) and therefore does not use the literal senses listed; رسید is a perfect/past «arrive/reach» meaning “reached / came to.” The whole clause means “when Saveh’s words reached Rostam / when Rostam heard Saveh’s words.”
segments.s229-g12: بزد is the simple past third‑person verb “he struck / smote.” دست is the noun “hand” and functions as the object of بزد. و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” گرز گران is a noun phrase “heavy mace” with the adjective گران “heavy/weighty,” and برکشید is a past tense verb “he lifted / raised.” The sequence describes two successive actions: he struck (with his hand) and then raised his heavy mace.
segments.s229-g13: بزد is past “he struck.” بر سرش is a prepositional phrase “upon his head” (سر + possessive ش). گرز را is the definite direct object marked by را (“the mace”), and پیلتن is an adverbial/place name “at Piltan” modifying the action. Word order places the place-name after the object for emphasis.
segments.s229-g14: که introduces a result/consequence clause “so that / whereby.” جانش is “his life” (جان + possessive ش). برون شد is a verbal phrase “went out / came forth” (برون ‘out, forth’ + شد ‘became/happened’). بزاری here conveys the sense of lamentation or a cry (a wailing associated with death), ز is the preposition “from,” and تن is “the body.” The clause reports the result of the blow: his life left his body with a cry.
segments.s229-g15: برآورد here is a verbal preterite that in context means roughly “he lifted up / took up” (the exact poetic usage is archaic). و links the next verb; زد is past “he struck / smote.” بر سر و مغفرش is the compound prepositional phrase “upon (his) head and its covering/helmet” (مغفر = covering/helmet, + possessive ش). The sequence describes lifting and then delivering a blow to the head and helmet.
segments.s229-g16: ندیدست is a perfect/passive‑like expression meaning “was not seen / has not been seen.” گفتی in this line functions idiomatically as “one might say / so to speak” (the literal senses given in the list do not match this idiomatic use). تنش را سرش strings together body and head (تنِش = his body, را = object marker, سرش = his head) and is a verbless clause meaning roughly “his body and head (were) not seen.” Altogether this yields a rhetorical remark that his body and head could hardly be discerned.
segments.s229-g17: بیفگند is a perfective/past narrative verb (‘he threw/cast’) used here without an explicit object (the object is understood from context). و simply links the two clauses. رخش (’his steed’) is the noun subject of the following verb; از بر او is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘from his side/presence’ (از = ‘from’, بر here part of the fixed phrase از بر = ‘from the side of’); براند is a past/resultative form meaning ‘drove/removed/carried away’ and so completes the verb phrase describing what the steed did.
segments.s229-g18: ز is the poetic form of از (‘from’). ساوه is the place-name Saveh. بگیتی is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘in the world’ (ب- prefixed to گیتی). نشانی is the noun ‘sign/trace’, and نماند is the past negative verb ‘did not remain / was left no more’, so the whole clause says that no trace of Saveh remained in the world.
segments.s229-g19: درفش کشانی is a noun phrase (‘banner/standard‑bearing [men]’), where کشانی is a participial/adjectival form from کشاند meaning ‘carrying/bringing’ and identifies the bearers. نگونسار is an adjective meaning ‘woeful/mournful’ and کرد is the causative/past verb ‘made/caused’, so the clause means ‘he made the standard‑bearers mournful’. The verb کرد causatively links the adjective to the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s229-g20: Initial و is the simple coordinator ‘and’. ز + و here combine as زو (از + او) meaning ‘from him/through him’ or ‘by him’ (indicating cause). جان لشکر is a genitive construction ‘the life(s) of the army’ (i.e. the soldiers’ lives). پرآزار is an adjective ‘full of harm/very hurtful’ and کرد is the past causative ‘he caused’, so the clause says he caused grievous harm to the army’s lives.
segments.s229-g21: نبد is an archaic negative past form meaning ‘there was not / did not exist’. نیز functions here as an additive/emphatic particle (‘also / even’). کس is ‘anyone/person’ but with the preceding negative it yields ‘no one’. پیش او means ‘in his presence / before him’, and پایدار is an adjective ‘steady/able to stand’, so together the clause says that no one remained steady before him.
segments.s229-g22: همه is the universal quantifier ‘all/every’. خاک here is used as ‘dust/earth’ and modifies مغز سر (’the brain of the head’) as a metaphoric object: مغز (brain) + سر (head). آورد is the past verb ‘brought/caused’ and بار literally ‘a load/burden’ (used idiomatically here); the clause is a poetic way of saying he reduced every head’s brain to dust or put every brain as a burden on the earth (i.e. killed them).
segments.s229-g23: پس is an adverb ‘then / thereafter’. از میمنه indicates movement ‘from the right side/flank’ (میمنه = the right side). شد is the past auxiliary/verb ‘became / turned (one’s direction)’. سوی is the preposition ‘toward’ and میسره here refers to the opposite flank (left side) — in epic usage میمنه/میسره name the two sides, so the clause says he shifted/took his course from the right toward the left flank.
segments.s229-g24: غمی is the noun ‘grief/sorrow’ used as the predicate subject. گشت is the past of گشتن ‘became / turned into’. لشکر is the army/host; همه is ‘all/everyone’; یکسره is an adverb/adjective meaning ‘completely / outright’. Together the clause states that grief came upon the whole army and they were altogether stricken.
segments.s229-g1: یکی خویش: 'یکی' is the indefinite 'one/a certain' and 'خویش' here means 'one of his own / his kinsman or follower' (poetic compactness; the possessor is implied). کاموس is a proper name (here the person being introduced). بد ساوه نام: 'بد' is an epithet 'bad/ill/rough' placed before the following name, and 'ساوه نام' literally 'named Saaveh' (نام after the name is a postpositive 'called X'). Word order and omitted ezâfe (possessive/linking particle) are typical of epic Persian.
segments.s229-g2: سرافراز و هر جای گسترده کام: 'سرافراز' is an adjectival epithet 'lofty/proud.' 'و' links clauses. 'هر جای' = 'every place/wherever,' and 'گسترده' (here) qualifies 'کام' — 'کام گسترده' — meaning his desire/ambition was spread far and wide. The adjective/noun order and omission of particles are poetic.
segments.s229-g3: بیامد بپیش تهمتن بجنگ: 'بیامد' is past tense 'he came.' 'بپیش' (= به پیش) means 'toward / into the presence of.' 'تهمتن' is a proper name/epithet (Tahmātan, i.e. Rostam). 'بجنگ' is the infinitive 'to fight' (poetic fused form). Together the clause means 'he came up toward Tahmatan to fight.'
segments.s229-g4: یکی تیغ هندی گرفته بچنگ: 'یکی' = 'a certain one/someone.' 'تیغ هندی' = 'an Indian sword' (noun + adjective). 'گرفته' is the past participle 'having taken / holding,' and 'بچنگ' = 'into the grasp / in hand.' The construction describes someone who has seized an Indian sword and holds it.
segments.s229-g5: بگردید گرد چپ و دست راست: 'بگردید' (classical inflected form of گشتن) here means 'turned / looked around.' 'گرد' = 'around,' and 'چپ و دست راست' = 'to the left and to the right hand(s).' The phrase describes scanning/looking about in both directions; the verb form is poetic/archaic but functions as simple past here.
segments.s229-g6: ز رستم همی کین کاموس خواست: 'ز' (poetic for از) = 'against / from' here marking the object of vengeance. 'رستم' is the target name. 'همی' is a classical prefix giving ongoing/emphatic aspect (often glossed 'did/was'), and 'کین' = 'vengeance/hatred.' 'کاموس' is the subject (a proper name) and 'خواست' is the past 'sought/wanted.' Word order is inverted (object before subject+verb), so: 'Kāmos sought vengeance against Rostam.'
segments.s229-g7: برستم چنین گفت کای ژنده پیل: 'برستم' is a fused poetic form equivalent to 'به رستم' = 'to Rostam' (addressing Rostam). 'چنین گفت' = 'thus he said.' 'کای' is the vocative 'O!' and 'ژنده پیل' is an insulting vocative 'ragged/filthy elephant' (ژنده = rag/ragged, پیل = elephant) used metaphorically to address a large man. The clause introduces direct speech.
segments.s229-g8: ببینی کنون موج دریای نیل: 'ببینی' is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive/imperative form meaning 'see!' or 'do you see' (classical/poetic form). 'کنون' = 'now.' 'موج دریای نیل' is a genitive chain: 'the wave of the sea of the Nile' = 'the waves of the Nile.' The speaker asks rhetorically whether the addressee now sees the Nile's waves.

sentences.s229-s5: Now I wish to take vengeance on the sea‑devourer, if I am to mount the saddle for battle again.
sentences.s229-s6: When the words of Saveh reached Rostam, he struck with his hand and lifted his heavy mace.
sentences.s229-s7: He struck his head with the mace at Piltan, so that his life went forth from his body with a cry.
sentences.s229-s8: He lifted (him) up and smote upon the head and its helmet; one might say his body and head were not to be seen.
sentences.s229-s9: He cast (him) away, and his steed carried him from his side; from Saveh no trace remained in the world.
sentences.s229-s10: He made the standard‑bearers mournful, and by him he caused great suffering to the army’s life.
sentences.s229-s11: There was no one, either, who could stand firm before him; he reduced every head’s brain to dust (i.e. he slaughtered them all).
sentences.s229-s12: Then from the right flank he turned toward the left; sorrow fell upon the army — the whole host was utterly bereaved.
sentences.s229-s1: One of his men, Kāmos called Saaveh, was proud, and his desires extended everywhere.
sentences.s229-s2: He came forward to fight Tahmatan; a certain man had taken an Indian sword into his grasp.
sentences.s229-s3: He looked around to the left and to the right; Kāmos sought revenge against Rostam.
sentences.s229-s4: To Rostam he said thus, 'O ragged elephant, do you now see the waves of the Nile?'
